title: Practice Test: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar / ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping / The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.

passages.title: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar
passages.sourceTitle: Et tu, Brute? The real story of Brutus’ betrayal of Caesar
passages.contentHtml:
A Marcus Junius Brutus was a Roman politician, leader, orator—and one of history’s most infamous assassins. Despite Julius Caesar having spared his life, Brutus murdered him. In Caesar’s final moments, surrounded by conspirators, there was no more painful stab wound than that inflicted by Brutus.

B Brutus was born the son of a patrician and a wealthy plebeian. His mother, Servilia, maintained a long, romantic relationship with Caesar. Some historians speculate that Caesar might have fathered Brutus, but this seems unlikely.

C In his first act of disloyalty, Brutus became a political supporter of Pompey against Caesar. Plutarch insists that Brutus acted out of political conviction, believing that Pompey represented a more just option against tyranny.

D When Caesar defeated Pompey, Brutus’s life was spared by Caesar, who pardoned him. For the next four years, Brutus enjoyed Caesar’s favor and was made governor of Cisalpine Gaul.

E By 44 B.C., Brutus was a praetor, thanks to Caesar’s support. However, he was drawn into the conspiracy to assassinate Caesar by his brother-in-law, Gaius Cassius Longinus.

F The conspiracy succeeded in taking Caesar’s life, but it failed to restore the Roman Republic. The senators did not support the assassins, and Brutus was unable to deliver his prepared speech.

G The aftermath of the assassination was chaotic. Mark Antony, Caesar’s lieutenant, fled, and the conspirators took refuge on Capitoline Hill. Cicero advised Brutus to convene the Senate, but Brutus did not follow this advice.

H Eventually, Antony’s proposal for amnesty and concord was approved, calming the situation. The conspirators were exonerated, but Caesar’s decisions were ratified, preventing further unrest.

passages.title: ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping
passages.sourceTitle: ‘Rescuing’ baby animals may amount to kidnapping
passages.contentHtml:
A Each spring, wildlife rescuers face a surge of baby animals brought in by well-meaning people who mistakenly believe the animals are abandoned.

B Connie Hall, who runs Magnolia Fawn Rescue, recounts a case where a fawn died after being taken from the wild, highlighting the dangers of such 'rescues.'

C Many animals, like deer and rabbits, leave their young alone to protect them from predators. People often mistake this for abandonment.

D The problem is widespread, with thousands of baby animals being taken from their natural habitats each year, often leading to their death.

E Baby animals like fawns and rabbits need specific care and nutrients from their mothers, which cannot be replicated by human intervention.

F Stress and improper care can lead to fatal conditions in these animals, such as capture myopathy and metabolic bone disease.

G In some regions, legal restrictions prevent the rehabilitation of certain animals, leading to euthanasia if they are taken from the wild.

H The article emphasizes the importance of leaving baby animals in their natural environment unless they are clearly in distress or injured.

passages.title: The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.
passages.sourceTitle: The Messier Marathon is coming. Here’s how to see 110 deep-sky wonders in one night.
passages.contentHtml:
A Each spring, amateur astronomers take on the challenge of the Messier Marathon, attempting to spot all 110 objects in Messier’s catalog in one night.

B The marathon is possible only during a short window in late March or early April, when all objects can be seen from dusk till dawn.

C Charles Messier’s catalog, created in 1774, includes famous objects like the Andromeda Galaxy and the Orion Nebula, visible with modest telescopes.

D Completing the marathon requires careful planning and stamina, even with modern equipment like GoTo telescopes.

E The best time for the marathon is during a new moon, which allows for better visibility of faint objects.

F Preparation involves choosing a good observation site and planning the order of observations to catch objects before they set.

G The marathon is primarily a Northern Hemisphere event, and local astronomy groups often host events to support participants.

H Stargazers are advised to start early and be prepared for real-time challenges like cloud cover during the marathon.
